title: Neural Latency Test
shortDescription: Measure your synaptic transmission speed in milliseconds.
description: A clinical-grade assessment of your visual cortex processing speed and motor neuron response time. Used by elite athletes and neuroscience researchers worldwide.

seo.metaTitle: Neural Latency Test – Free Online Reaction Time Benchmark
seo.metaDescription: Measure your reaction time with clinical precision. Click when green appears and benchmark your neural response speed against thousands of high-performers worldwide. Free, instant, no signup.

howToPlay: Click or tap anywhere on the stimulus panel to initiate the protocol.
The panel will display a red standby indicator — remain focused and do not interact.
The moment the panel switches to green, click or tap as fast as possible.
Your Neural Latency score is displayed in milliseconds (ms).
Share your Neuro-Bench Report Card and challenge your colleagues.
content.science: Neural latency — commonly called reaction time — measures the total elapsed time from sensory stimulus to motor response initiation. The signal travels from your retina through the optic nerve to the primary visual cortex (V1), is processed by the dorsal visual stream, then a motor command is dispatched through the corticospinal tract to your finger flexor muscles. The entire cascade takes approximately 150–300ms in healthy adults. Elite athletes, fighter pilots, and professional gamers consistently score below 200ms through deliberate neuromuscular training. Fatigue, dehydration, alcohol, and age all measurably degrade performance. A single well-rested morning session after caffeine intake typically produces a subject's personal best scores, as adrenergic stimulation accelerates cortical processing speed.
tips: Use your dominant index finger — it has the most direct motor pathway to the primary motor cortex.
Do not attempt to anticipate the green signal — pre-motor activation invalidates the measurement.
Perform 1–2 warm-up taps before beginning to prime your neuromuscular junction.
A wired input device eliminates Bluetooth polling latency (~8ms) for the most accurate score.
Controlled diaphragmatic breathing before the test measurably lowers sympathetic arousal and improves baseline latency.

title: Office Avenger
shortDescription: Rapidly neutralize workplace stressors. 10 taps. Maximum catharsis.
description: A proprietary stress-relief velocity assessment disguised as a clinical impulse response test. Tap the workplace aggressor 10 times as fast as neurologically possible.

seo.metaTitle: Office Avenger – Stress Relief Speed Test | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How fast can you neutralize a workplace stressor? Tap the boss 10 times as fast as possible. Measure your stress-relief velocity and share your catharsis score. Free instant test.

howToPlay: Click or tap the 'Begin Protocol' button to initiate the stress-relief assessment.
A workplace stressor stimulus (👹) will appear on screen.
Rapidly tap the stimulus exactly 10 times with maximum neurological urgency.
Your Stress-Relief Velocity is measured in total elapsed milliseconds.
Lower score = faster catharsis = higher workplace survival probability.
content.science: Repetitive motor tapping tasks have been studied extensively in neuroscience as a measure of corticospinal tract efficiency and basal ganglia timing circuits. The speed at which a subject can execute rapid sequential taps reflects the integrity of the supplementary motor area (SMA) and pre-motor cortex, which coordinate timing and sequencing of voluntary movements. Interestingly, research in occupational psychology has found that humor-based stress displacement activities — including symbolic aggression against authority figures — measurably reduce cortisol levels and self-reported workplace stress. This test combines both domains: a genuine measure of motor tapping speed, wrapped in the cathartic framework of office-stress relief.
tips: Use alternating index and middle fingers for maximum tap frequency — a technique called 'trill tapping.'
Keep your wrist elevated and tap from the fingertip only, not the full finger, to reduce travel time.
On mobile, multi-touch with two fingers alternating is significantly faster than single-finger tapping.
Controlled aggression — channeling genuine frustration — has been shown to improve motor output speed by up to 12% in lab conditions.
Visualize your most stressful Monday morning meeting for optimal performance.

title: Memory Matrix
shortDescription: How many digits can your brain hold? Beat the 7-digit average.
description: A clinical measure of your working memory span. Numbers flash briefly — memorize them, then type them back. Each correct answer adds one more digit. How far can you go?

seo.metaTitle: Number Memory Test – Working Memory Capacity Benchmark | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How many digits can you memorize? Test your working memory span online for free. The average person remembers 7 digits — can you beat it? Instant results, no signup.

howToPlay: Click 'Begin Protocol' to start the assessment.
A number sequence will flash on screen — memorize it before it disappears.
Type the number exactly as shown using the on-screen keypad or your keyboard.
Each correct answer adds one more digit to the next sequence.
One mistake ends the test — your score is the longest sequence you recalled correctly.
content.science: Working memory — the brain's mental scratchpad — is one of the most studied constructs in cognitive neuroscience. The digit span task, first formalized by psychologist George Miller in 1956, revealed that humans can hold approximately 7 ± 2 items in short-term memory at once, a finding now known as 'Miller's Law.' The prefrontal cortex and parietal lobes coordinate to maintain and manipulate these temporary representations. Working memory capacity strongly predicts academic performance, fluid intelligence, and professional decision-making speed. Unlike long-term memory, working memory degrades rapidly without active rehearsal — typically within 15–30 seconds. Elite performers in high-stakes professions (surgeons, air traffic controllers, chess grandmasters) consistently score above 9 digits, suggesting working memory is a trainable cognitive resource.
tips: Chunk the digits into groups of 2–3 (e.g. '8 3 7 1 4' → '83' '71' '4') to dramatically extend your span.
Subvocalize the sequence — silently repeating numbers engages the phonological loop, a dedicated working memory subsystem.
Avoid distractions during the flash window — divided attention is the primary cause of encoding failure.
The display time scales with digit count, so longer sequences give you proportionally more time — don't rush.
Practice consistently: working memory span is one of the few cognitive metrics proven to improve with targeted training.

title: Sequence Memory
shortDescription: Memorize the pattern. Repeat it back. How long can you go?
description: A clinical assessment of your sequential working memory and pattern recognition. Watch the grid light up — then repeat the exact sequence. Each round adds one more step.

seo.metaTitle: Sequence Memory Test – Pattern Recognition Benchmark | NeuroBench
seo.metaDescription: How long can you follow a growing sequence? Watch the pattern, repeat it back, and benchmark your sequential memory against players worldwide. Free, instant, no signup.

howToPlay: Click 'Begin Protocol' to start the assessment.
Watch the grid — squares will light up in a sequence.
After the sequence finishes, click the squares in the exact same order.
Each correct round adds one more step to the sequence.
One wrong click ends the test — your score is the longest sequence you completed.
content.science: Sequential pattern memory — the ability to encode, retain, and reproduce ordered spatial information — relies on a distributed neural network involving the hippocampus, dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, and the supplementary motor area. This capacity is distinct from simple digit span and is more closely linked to procedural learning and implicit sequence acquisition. Research in cognitive neuroscience demonstrates that sequence memory underlies complex skills including musical performance, surgical technique, and programming. The visuospatial sketchpad, a component of Baddeley's working memory model, is the primary system engaged during this task. Sequence length at which performance breaks down is a reliable predictor of general fluid intelligence and attentional control.
tips: Verbalize the positions as you watch — 'top-left, center, bottom-right' creates a dual-coding memory trace.
Chunk the sequence into groups of 3 once it exceeds 5 steps, treating each chunk as a single unit.
Track the rhythm of the highlights — sequences have timing patterns your motor memory can exploit.
Avoid blinking during the display phase — even a 200ms blink can cause you to miss a flash.
After each round, mentally replay the full sequence before the next display begins to reinforce encoding.
